summary: Proto-oncogenes are normal genes that regulate cell growth; when mutated they become oncogenes that promote uncontrolled proliferation.
Oncogenes are normal genes that become tumour suppressors when mutated.
Proto-oncogenes are always harmful and must be silenced for normal cell function.
The RAS oncogene is mutated in approximately 30% of all human cancers, making it the most commonly mutated oncogene.
The RAS oncogene is mutated in less than 1% of human cancers.
RAS mutations are exclusively found in lung cancers.
p53 is called the guardian of the genome because it triggers apoptosis when DNA damage is irreparable.
p53 promotes cell division when DNA damage is detected.
p53 is an oncogene that drives tumour formation when activated.
HIV is a retrovirus that uses reverse transcriptase to convert its RNA genome into DNA before integrating into the host genome.
HIV is a DNA virus that directly integrates into the host genome without reverse transcription.
HIV uses DNA polymerase to replicate its genome independently of the host cell.
HIV primarily targets CD4+ T-helper cells, leading to progressive immunodeficiency and susceptibility to opportunistic infections.
HIV primarily targets B lymphocytes, causing antibody deficiency.
HIV infects red blood cells, causing anaemia as its primary pathology.
Ethanol is oxidised to acetaldehyde by alcohol dehydrogenase, and the resulting increase in NADH:NAD+ ratio causes fatty liver.
Ethanol is directly converted to acetic acid without any intermediate metabolite.
Alcohol dehydrogenase converts ethanol to formaldehyde, which causes liver damage.
The MEOS (microsomal ethanol oxidising system) involving CYP2E1 is induced by chronic alcohol consumption and generates reactive oxygen species.
The MEOS pathway is suppressed by chronic alcohol intake.
CYP2E1 is only active in the kidneys and plays no role in alcohol metabolism.
